summary: Kenneth Suna demonstrates the risks of selling cash-secured puts into earnings using a HIMS trade that went against him. He illustrates the decision framework when assignment is at risk: either buy back the contract and exit for a loss, or hold for assignment at the original strike price.
takeaways: Never trade into earnings volatility—too risky and unpredictable
When selling cash-secured puts, you must reserve the full strike price × shares in cash (e.g., $17,100 for 300 shares at $57 strike)
If assigned, you're forced to buy at strike price regardless of current market price—if HIMS is $51 but you sold $57 puts, you buy at $57
Buying back an options contract to exit early locks in losses but may be worth it if conviction changes or downside risk is severe
Assignment losses can exceed premium collected—he collected $814 but could lose $17,100 in capital if assigned and stock continues falling
The wheel strategy (covered calls + cash-secured puts) is not risk-free and experienced traders make mistakes trading into earnings

strategy_candidates.change: Skip Premium Desk income targets during earnings week—pause selling covered calls or cash-secured puts to preserve capital and avoid forced assignment/buyback losses
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly states: 'I probably shouldn't have done this. I probably just should have sat out this week' and demonstrates how trading HIMS into earnings created $786+ realized loss scenario and potential $17,100 assignment loss, directly undermining the consistent $1,250/month premium goal for safety.

title: I made a huge covered call mistake! Read the description for more
